0:0.rendering: The words of Nehemiah son of Hakaliah. In the month of Kislev, in the twentieth year, while I was in the citadel of Susa,
0:0.note: The Hebrew word for "words" here is the same opening as in the prophetic books, hinting that this memoir carries the weight of divine speech.

0:1.rendering: Hanani, one of my brothers, arrived from Judah with some other men, and I questioned them about the Jewish remnant who had survived the exile, and about Jerusalem.
0:1.note: The word "remnant" echoes the prophetic hope of Isaiah and Jeremiah that a small surviving group would become the seed of renewal.

0:2.rendering: They told me, "The remnant who are left from the captivity there in the province are in great trouble and shame. The wall of Jerusalem is broken down, and its gates have been burned with fire."
0:2.note: This report echoes the opening of Lamentations, painting Jerusalem's ruin as an ongoing exile wound.

0:3.rendering: When I heard these words, I sat down and wept. For days I mourned, fasted, and prayed before the God of heaven.
0:3.note: The Hebrew verb for "sat down" echoes Joshua's posture of stunned grief in Joshua 7:6 after Israel's defeat, linking Nehemiah's mourning to a tradition of leaders who grieve covenant failure.

0:4.rendering: I said, "O LORD, God of heaven, the great and awesome God who keeps covenant and steadfast love with those who love him and keep his commandments."
0:4.note: The Hebrew pairs "great and awesome" with the same phrase used for God's terrifying appearance at Sinai, reminding exiles that this fearsome God still honors his covenant.

0:5.rendering: Let your ear be attentive and your eyes open to hear the prayer of your servant that I am praying before you now, day and night, for the Israelites your servants, while I confess the sins of the Israelites that we have sinned against you. Both I and my father's house have sinned.
0:5.note: Nehemiah's confession echoes the covenant language of Deuteronomy, linking personal and communal guilt in one breath.

0:6.rendering: We have acted very corruptly against you. We have not kept the commandments, the statutes, and the ordinances that you commanded your servant Moses.
0:6.note: The triple repetition of "we have" echoes the threefold structure of Israel's covenant obligations, confessing a comprehensive break with the Torah given through Moses.

0:7.rendering: Remember the word that you commanded your servant Moses: "If you are unfaithful, I will scatter you among the peoples."
0:7.note: This echoes Deuteronomy 4:27 and 28:64, where Moses warned of exile for covenant-breaking, setting up Nehemiah's prayer as a bold appeal to the same ancient promise of restoration.

1:0.rendering: In the month of Nisan, in the twentieth year of King Artaxerxes, when wine was set before him, I took the wine and gave it to the king. Now I had never been sad in his presence before.
1:0.note: The Hebrew highlights a calculated shift: Nehemiah had always appeared cheerful before the king until this pivotal Nisan moment.

1:1.rendering: So the king said to me, "Why is your face sad when you are not sick? This is nothing but sadness of heart." Then I was very much afraid.
1:1.note: The king's sharp eye for Nehemiah's hidden grief reveals the high-stakes court culture where showing sorrow could mean disloyalty or even death.

1:2.rendering: May the king live forever. Why should my face not look sad when the city where my ancestors are buried lies in ruins and its gates have been destroyed by fire?
1:2.note: Nehemiah's opening words echo the ancient courtly formula for addressing a Persian king, then pivot to raw grief over Jerusalem's broken walls.

1:3.rendering: Then the king said to me, "What are you requesting?" So I prayed to the God of heaven.
1:3.note: Nehemiah's split-second prayer before answering shows the habit of turning to God in the middle of a high-stakes conversation.

1:4.rendering: Then I said to the king, "If it pleases the king, and if your servant has found favor in your sight, send me to Judah, to the city of my ancestors' tombs, so that I may rebuild it."
1:4.note: Nehemiah's bold request echoes the ancient Near Eastern custom of kings granting favor for ancestral tomb restoration, turning personal grief into communal renewal.

1:5.rendering: The king said to me, while the queen sat beside him, "How long will your journey take, and when will you return?" So it pleased the king to send me, and I set a time for him.
1:5.note: The queen's silent presence at this pivotal meal hints at Persian court protocol, where royal women witnessed key decisions, framing Nehemiah's bold request as a family affair rather than mere politics.

1:6.rendering: Then I said to the king, If it pleases the king, let letters be given me to the governors of the province Beyond the River, that they may let me pass through until I come to Judah.
1:6.note: The phrase "Beyond the River" echoes the Persian imperial title for the vast western territory, showing how Nehemiah shrewdly uses the empire's own administrative language to secure safe passage.

1:7.rendering: And a letter to Asaph, the keeper of the king's park, that he may give me timber to make beams for the gates of the fortress that belongs to the temple, and for the wall of the city, and for the house that I shall occupy. And the king granted me this, according to the good hand of my God upon me.
1:7.note: The Persian word for "park" here is pardes, the root of our word "paradise," an echo of the garden God planted in Eden that Nehemiah now seeks to restore.

0:8.rendering: But if you return to me and keep my commands and do them, then even if your banished ones are at the farthest horizon, I will gather them from there and bring them to the place I have chosen as a dwelling for my name.
0:8.note: This promise echoes Deuteronomy 30:1-5 almost word for word, showing how Nehemiah reads Israel's story through the lens of Moses' ancient covenant.

0:9.rendering: They are your servants and your people, whom you redeemed by your great strength and your mighty hand.
0:9.note: The Hebrew word for "redeemed" echoes God's rescue of Israel from Egypt, reminding exiles that the same powerful hand that split the sea can bring them home again.

0:10.rendering: O Lord, please let your ear be attentive to the prayer of your servant and to the prayer of your servants who delight in fearing your name. Grant success to your servant today, and make him find favor in the sight of this man. I was cupbearer to the king.
0:10.note: The Hebrew word for "success" here is the same one used for Joshua's prosperity in Joshua 1:8, quietly echoing that ancient call to lead God's people into their inheritance.

1:8.rendering: Then I came to the governors of the province Beyond the River and gave them the letters from the king. Now the king had sent army captains and horsemen with me.
1:8.note: The Persian title "Beyond the River" echoes the very imperial machinery that once exiled Judah, now ironically funding its return.

1:9.rendering: When Sanballat the Horonite and Tobiah the Ammonite official heard this, it displeased them greatly that someone had come to seek the welfare of the people of Israel.
1:9.note: Sanballat and Tobiah's rage reveals how rebuilding Jerusalem's walls threatened the power structures of the surrounding provinces.

1:10.rendering: So I came to Jerusalem. I was there three days.
1:10.note: The three-day pause echoes ancient travel rituals, giving Nehemiah time to observe before acting.

1:11.rendering: I arose at night, I and a few men with me. I told no one what my God had put in my heart to do for Jerusalem. There was no animal with me except the one on which I rode.
1:11.note: The nighttime ride and secret mission echo the stealth of Joshua's spies, showing how God stirs the heart before the walls go up.

1:12.rendering: I went out by night through the Valley Gate toward the Dragon Spring and to the Dung Gate, and I inspected the walls of Jerusalem that were broken down and whose gates had been destroyed by fire.
1:12.note: Nehemiah's nighttime inspection deliberately echoes the spies' secret reconnaissance in Numbers 13, but this time the report will fuel courageous rebuilding instead of fear.

1:13.rendering: Then I went on to the Fountain Gate and to the King's Pool, but there was no room for the animal under me to pass.
1:13.note: The Hebrew word for "room" (maqom) echoes the earlier search for a "place" to rebuild, underscoring how even the king's own pool lay blocked amid the rubble.

1:14.rendering: So I went up by the valley at night and inspected the wall. I turned back and entered by the Valley Gate, and so returned.
1:14.note: The Hebrew word for "inspected" here is the same one used for "examining" a sacrificial animal, quietly portraying Nehemiah as both surveyor and priestly inspector of his broken city.

1:15.rendering: The officials did not know where I had gone or what I was doing. I had not yet told the Jews, the priests, the nobles, the officials, or the rest who would do the work.
1:15.note: Nehemiah keeps his plans tightly guarded from even the local leaders until the moment is right, echoing the wisdom of a man who acts only after careful reconnaissance and prayer.

1:16.rendering: Then I said to them, "You see the trouble we are in, how Jerusalem lies in ruins with its gates burned. Come, let us rebuild the wall of Jerusalem, that we may no longer be a reproach."
1:16.note: Nehemiah's "you see the trouble" echoes Moses' urgent calls to action in Exodus, rallying a scattered people to reverse exile's shame together.

1:17.rendering: Then I told them how the good hand of my God had been upon me, and also the words that the king had spoken to me. They replied, "Let us rise up and build." So they strengthened their hands for the good work.
1:17.note: The phrase "good hand of my God" echoes Ezra 7:9 and 8:22, quietly signaling that this building project belongs to the same divine story of return from exile.

1:18.rendering: When Sanballat the Horonite, Tobiah the Ammonite official, and Geshem the Arab heard this, they mocked us and despised us. They said, "What is this thing you are doing? Are you rebelling against the king?"
1:18.note: These three outsiders, each tied to regions long hostile to Israel, instantly frame the wall as treason, echoing centuries of imperial suspicion in the ancient Near East.

1:19.rendering: Then I answered them and said to them, "The God of heaven will give us success. We his servants will arise and build. But you have no share, no right, and no memorial in Jerusalem."
1:19.note: Nehemiah's sharp reply echoes the ancient covenant language of "portion and inheritance," denying the opponents any stake in the sacred city that God has claimed for his people.
